placeholder.pgnInput: Paste PGN or a move list (e.g. e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6)…

empty.moveHistoryCommentator: Moves appear here as you step through the game.
empty.insights: Step through the moves to see the engine's take.

ui.practiceLede: Play against the computer. The coach whispers tips as you go, and you can always ask the AI Coach for deeper help.

toast.pasteFirst: Paste PGN or a move list first.

brilliant.label: Brilliant move
brilliant.comment: A brilliant find — a sacrifice the engine still rates as the strongest move on the board.

best.label: Best move
best.comment: This is the engine's top pick — no better move on the board.

good.label: Solid
good.commentAlt: A reasonable continuation. The engine slightly preferred {best}.

inaccuracy.label: Inaccuracy
inaccuracy.comment: Small slip — {best} would have been a touch stronger.

hint.castle: Castles {dir} — tucks the king behind a wall of pawns.
hint.promote: Pushes to the last rank and promotes to a {piece}.
hint.develop: Develops your {piece} to {square}, improving activity and eyeing the center.
hint.rook: Activates your rook on {square} — open files love rooks.

coach.greeting: Hi — I'm your chess coach. Ask me anything about the position. I explain ideas, tactics, and plans.
coach.system: You are KarpaChess, an upbeat, expert chess coach. Explain ideas in clear, vivid, encouraging language for a learner. Be concise: 2–4 short paragraphs, or a tight bulleted list. Ground claims in the current position. Use SAN for moves. When recommending a move, briefly explain WHY. Never hallucinate pieces or squares that aren't present. Reply in {language}.

whisper.tip1: Nice — now what about your next piece?
whisper.tip2: Every move, ask: am I improving my worst piece?
whisper.tip3: Watch for forks and tactics after each exchange.
whisper.tip4: Keep your king safe. Castle if you haven't.
whisper.tip5: Look for pieces without defenders — yours and theirs.
whisper.castle: 🏰 Nicely castled! King safety matters. Eval: {eval}.
whisper.welcome: Welcome! Make a move and I'll share a quick insight. When the game ends I'll offer to review your moves.
whisper.newGame: New game! Good luck — make the first move.

bestMove.castle: • King still in the center — castling is a top priority.
bestMove.develop: • Minor pieces still on {squares} — develop them.
bestMove.center: • No pawn on the central four — a central pawn push is valuable.
bestMove.loose: • Opponent has {pieceWord}: {list}. Look for ways to win them.
bestMove.oppKing: • Opponent's king is {score} — attacking moves often work.
bestMove.ownKing: • Your king is under fire — consider defense first.
bestMove.activeKing: • Activate your king — in endgames, he's a fighting piece.

tactics.defended: Opponent's pieces look defended. Harder tactics — look for pins, decoys, or clearance moves.
tactics.exposedKing: Their king is {score} ({notes}) — mating patterns may be lurking.
tactics.queenTarget: Their queen on {square} — can you attack her cheaply to gain tempo?
tactics.engineSuggests: Engine suggests {move} — does that move fit any of the patterns above?
tactics.footer: _Train your eye to spot these three every move: loose pieces, exposed kings, overworked defenders._

plan.queenEarly: Careful — queen moved before all minor pieces were developed. Don't let her be chased.
plan.centerPawn: Put a pawn on the central four (d4/e4/d5/e5).
plan.kingDanger: Your king is in danger — improve defense before attacking.
plan.oppositeWings: You both castled — attack the opposite wing with pawns if castled on opposite sides.
plan.isolated: Watch your {pawnWord} on {squares} — activity compensates for weakness.
plan.improveWorst: Improve your worst piece. Every move should make one of your pieces better.
plan.activateKing: Activate your king — walk him into the action.
plan.aheadTrade: You're up {n} points — trade pieces (not pawns) to simplify to a winning endgame.
plan.behindKeep: You're down {n} points — keep pieces on the board, create counterplay, avoid trades.
plan.fewPawns: Few pawns left — think about opposition, zugzwang, and key squares.

lastMove.none: No moves played yet — start with a central pawn (e4 or d4) or a knight move (Nf3).

opening.noneE4: 1.e4 — opens lines for queen and bishop, fights for the center. Leads to sharp tactical play.
opening.noneD4: 1.d4 — claims the center, prepares c4 (Queen's Gambit). Strategic, positional.
opening.noneNf3: 1.Nf3 — flexible, delays revealing your plan. Often transposes.
opening.noneC4: 1.c4 — English. Flank attack on the center, hypermodern style.
opening.nonePick: Pick one, play it a hundred times, and learn its secrets.
opening.unknownHeader: {n} {moveWord} played — no clear opening match yet. Stick to three principles:
opening.unknownP1: • Control the center with pawns or pieces
opening.unknownP2: • Develop every minor piece before moving the queen

endgame.activateKing: • Activate your king — in the endgame, he's worth about three pawns. Walk him into the center.
endgame.passed: • Passed pawns are gold. White has {w}; Black has {b}.
endgame.opposition: • Opposition decides king-and-pawn endgames — the side not to move often wins the key square.
endgame.rookRule: • Rook endings follow the rule: rook belongs BEHIND the passed pawn (yours or theirs).
endgame.tradePieces: • When winning, trade pieces not pawns. When losing, keep pieces and seek tactics.

kingSafety.footer: If your king is exposed: defend first. If your opponent's is exposed: look for checks, rook lifts, and queen infiltration.

pawns.isolated: • Isolated: {squares} — hard to defend, but often active.
pawns.doubled: • Doubled on {files} — can't defend each other, often a long-term weakness.
pawns.clean: • Clean structure — no notable weaknesses or passed pawns.

development.openingNote: In the opening, every move should either (1) develop a piece, (2) control the center, or (3) improve king safety. Moves that don't do one of these are suspect.

improvement.intro: Look at each of your pieces and ask: "What is this doing?" Your goal each move is to give the least active piece a meaningful job.
improvement.bishop: • A bishop behind its own pawns — reroute or trade.
improvement.knight: • A knight on the rim (h3, a6 etc.) — centralize to d4/e4/d5/e5.
improvement.rook: • A rook on its starting square with no open file — find one and invade.
improvement.queen: • The queen too early and exposed — bring her back to safety first.
improvement.kingMid: • A king still in the center past move 10 — castle immediately.

summary.menu: Ask me: best move · tactics · plan · king safety · pawns · development · evaluation

kingNote.tuckedCorner: still tucked in the corner — centralize him
kingNote.startingSquare: king on his starting square — walk him out
kingNote.centerAfter8: king still in the center after move 8
kingNote.enemyHeavy: enemy heavy piece on the king's file
kingNote.fullShield: castled with a full pawn shield

openingTips.ruyLopez: The oldest serious opening in chess. The Bb5 pins the knight — Black usually plays ...a6 to force a decision. After Ba4, play develops into long strategic battles.
openingTips.ruyLopezMorphy: Black's main response. White usually chooses between the Main Line (Ba4 Nf6 0-0) and the Exchange Variation (Bxc6). Rich, classical chess awaits.
openingTips.italianGame: Bc4 targets f7 — the weakest point in Black's camp. Develop knights to f3/c3, castle kingside, and look for d3/d4 breaks.
openingTips.italianGiuocoPiano: "Quiet game" — both bishops point at each king's weak square. Classical setup: develop, castle, then push d4.
openingTips.italianTwoKnights: Sharp play! White can try the Fried Liver (Ng5) or the quieter d4 break. Black has to navigate some tactical waters.
openingTips.petroff: Rock-solid symmetric defense. Black copies White and develops naturally — notorious for its drawish tendencies at high levels.
openingTips.scotchGame: White opens the center early with d4. Active play for both sides, less theoretical than the Ruy.
openingTips.viennaGame: Develops Nc3 before Nf3, keeping f-pawn free for attacking possibilities (f4 later).
openingTips.kingsGambit: Romantic-era sacrifice! White offers the f-pawn for rapid development and attacks on f7. Rare but thrilling.
openingTips.openGame: Classical chess at its finest. Expect principled, center-based play.
openingTips.kingsPawnGame: Both sides claim central space with pawns. Sharp, direct chess is ahead.
openingTips.sicilian: The most popular answer to 1.e4. Asymmetric — White attacks on the kingside, Black counter-attacks on the queenside. Sharp, decisive games.
openingTips.sicilianNajdorf: The ultimate fighting opening. Every move is rich in theory. Prepare for dynamic, unbalanced play.
openingTips.sicilianOpenNc6: Classical Sicilian. Expect a race between White's kingside attack and Black's queenside counter.
openingTips.sicilianTaimanovKan: Flexible, modern Sicilian. Black keeps options open for ...a6, ...b5 expansion.
openingTips.sicilianClosed: White avoids the Open Sicilian with Nc3. Slower, more positional game. Avoid if you love tactics.
openingTips.french: Black builds a granite pawn chain. Look for ...c5 or ...f6 breaks to free the light-squared bishop.
openingTips.caroKann: Solid and flexible. ...d5 is coming next with full central counterplay.
openingTips.scandinavian: Black attacks the e4 pawn immediately. After Nc3 the queen gets chased — careful with her placement.
openingTips.pirc: Hypermodern. Black invites White to build a big center, then strikes it with pawn breaks.
openingTips.modern: Similar to the Pirc but even more flexible. Black delays committing to a setup.
openingTips.alekhine: Provocative! Black lures White's pawns forward, then attacks them. Sharp and unconventional.
openingTips.queensGambit: Offer the c-pawn to deflect Black's d-pawn and dominate the center. Classical, principled, top-tier.
openingTips.queensGambitDeclined: Black refuses the pawn and builds a solid structure. The quintessential positional opening.
openingTips.queensGambitAccepted: Black takes the pawn, plans to give it back for active piece play.
openingTips.slav: Supports the d5 pawn with ...c6. Solid, hard to break down, keeps the bishop on c8 alive.
openingTips.londonSetup: White's universal system — Bf4 and stable development. Less theory, reliable results.
openingTips.london: Solid and easy to learn. Claim d4, develop Bf4, play Nf3, Nbd2, c3, e3. Stable foundation.
openingTips.kingsIndian: Hypermodern classic. Let White build a huge center, then blast it with ...e5 or ...c5. Kingside attacks are legendary.
openingTips.nimzoIndian: Black pins the knight with Bb4 and plays to double White's pawns on c-file. Strategic, high-class chess.
openingTips.queensIndian: Solid, fianchetto-based setup. Popular at master level for its drawing chances.
openingTips.benoni: Sharp, unbalanced, tactical. Black gives up the center for activity.
openingTips.indianGame: Hypermodern response. Wait to see White's plan before committing.
openingTips.dutch: Black plays ...f5 to fight for e4. Sharp, double-edged. Careful with king safety!
openingTips.english: Flexible flank opening. Often transposes into Queen's Gambit or King's Indian structures. Hard for Black to prepare against.
openingTips.reti: Hypermodern — attack the center from the flanks. Great if you love positional play.
openingTips.retiKia: Flexible — can transpose into the King's Indian Attack or other closed setups.
openingTips.larsen: Fianchetto the queen's bishop early. Unusual but sound.
openingTips.sokolsky: Bb2 fianchetto with b4 thrown in. Offbeat — catch your opponent unprepared!
openingTips.benko: Quiet fianchetto system. Flexible, low-theory, positional.
openingTips.generic: Focus on the ideas — piece placement, pawn breaks, typical endgames.

settings.title: Settings
settings.aiNote: Keys are stored locally in your browser. Calls go from your browser directly to the provider.
settings.resetConfirm: Reset all progress, keys, and settings?

providerNote.builtin: No configuration needed — the built-in coach runs fully offline using local heuristics and the built-in engine.
providerNote.lmstudio: Load a model in LM Studio and start its local server. Pick one of the presets below, or type the exact model identifier LM Studio shows on its server page.
providerNote.openrouter: One key, 100+ models from Anthropic, OpenAI, Meta, Mistral, DeepSeek, xAI, Google, and more.
providerNote.glm: Zhipu AI's GLM models. The default endpoint is the China region — swap to a proxy if needed.
